metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the Exp B inclusion criterion to '≥3 top-level ## sections' (or adjust to 2 permutations per 2-section plan and update the AC prove script to allow n≥2), resolving the contradiction between the '≥2 sections' gate and the '≥3 permutations' AC requirement.
evidence: Inclusion criterion: 'of the 19, 15 have ≥2 top-level ## sections (the permutable set); the 4 single-section plans are excluded'
Mechanism: 'a pure helper permute_sections generates 3 section-order permutations by reordering the plan's top-level ## section blocks'
AC prove script: 'assert len(c)>=15 and all(n>=3 for n in c.values())'
A plan with exactly 2 sections has 2! = 2 distinct orderings; a third permutation would be a duplicate of the original or first swap.
findings.finding: The Exp B inclusion criterion is '≥2 top-level ## sections' but the AC prove script requires all(n>=3 for n in c.values()) — i.e. ≥3 distinct permutations per plan. A plan with exactly 2 sections has only 2 distinct orderings, so the harness either cannot satisfy the AC for such plans or must emit a duplicate permutation, which would artificially inflate agreement.
findings.location: Experiment B — 'Mechanism' subsection ('≥2 top-level ## sections' inclusion criterion) vs. AC 'Exp B run recorded' prove script (all(n>=3 for n in c.values()))
scenarios: Any of the 15 selected plans has exactly 2 top-level sections → permute_sections can only emit 2 distinct permutations → the AC prove assertion fails, or the harness emits a duplicate permutation inflating agreement.
The implementer silently repeats one permutation to satisfy the AC, making the κ/raw-agreement computation unreliable for that plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the inclusion criterion to '≥3 top-level ## sections' and update the prove script to `all(n>=2 for n in c.values())` with a note that plans with exactly 3 sections contribute 3! = 6 distinct permutations, so 3 are sampled, while plans with exactly 2 sections are either excluded or contribute 2 permutations. Alternatively, keep ≥2 sections and change the AC to `all(n>=2 for n in c.values())`, accepting that 2-section plans contribute 2 permutations and adjusting the Fleiss' κ computation to handle unequal rater counts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Exp A pseudocode to pass the inner verify dict to `pass3_decide`: replace `v = three_pass.pass2_verify(...)` / `pass3_decide(v, ...)` with `raw = three_pass.pass2_verify(...)` / `pass3_decide(raw["verify"], ...)` (or extract the inner dict before calling pass3).
evidence: three_pass.py:183-199: `pass2_verify` returns `{"verify": v, "mode": ..., "tool_calls": 0}` where `v` is the tool input dict
three_pass.py:283-318: `pass3_decide(verify, ...)` calls `verify.get("binary", {})` on its first arg — expects the inner dict, not the wrapper

three_pass.py:362-368 in `run_three_pass`: the batch path calls `pass3_decide(verifs.get(i), ...)` where `verifs` maps to inner verification dicts via `_verify_batch`; the single-vote agentic path diverges and wraps differently
findings.finding: The Exp A pseudocode passes `v` (the full `pass2_verify` return value `{"verify": {...}, "mode": ..., "tool_calls": ...}`) directly to `pass3_decide`, but `pass3_decide` expects the inner verify sub-dict (it calls `verify.get("binary", {})` and `verify.get("severity_attributes", {})`). This would silently treat the outer wrapper as the verify payload, causing every finding to score `confidence=0.0` and be dropped.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run pseudocode: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: When the harness executes the 3-vote loop using `pass2_verify` for each vote and passes its result directly to `pass3_decide`, `verify.get('binary', {})` returns `{}` (no 'binary' key in the outer wrapper), confidence=0.0, all 50 findings dropped, Fleiss' κ computed on all-'dropped' data — meaningless agreement metric.
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the pseudocode:
```python
raw = three_pass.pass2_verify(inp["title"], inp["plan"], inp,
                              repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT, agentic=True)
verify_dict = raw["verify"] if raw else None
d = three_pass.pass3_decide(verify_dict, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the '≥2 sections' vs '≥3 permutations' contradiction: either raise the inclusion criterion to '≥3 top-level sections' (which guarantees ≥3! = 6 distinct orderings), or change the AC to `all(n>=2 for n in c.values())`; and specify the permutation selection algorithm (e.g., 'original order + deterministic random sample of 2 others, fixed seed S').
evidence: Plan inclusion criterion: '15 have ≥2 top-level ## sections (the permutable set)'
Plan: 'a pure helper `permute_sections` generates 3 section-order permutations by reordering the plan's top-level ## section blocks'
AC prove-script: `assert len(c)>=15 and all(n>=3 for n in c.values())` — requires ≥3 permutations for every included plan
A plan with exactly 2 sections has 2! = 2 distinct orderings < 3 — the AC would fail; no resolution is stated
ABSENCE: no algorithm specified for selecting 3 from N! permutations
findings.finding: The Exp B section-permutation generation strategy is incompletely specified: the plan guarantees '3 section-order permutations' per plan, but never states how 3 permutations are selected from the combinatorial space. For a plan with N sections there are N! possible orderings; the plan neither specifies 'fixed seed random sample', 'first 3 in lexicographic order', 'original + two rotations', nor any other algorithm. The AC prove-script asserts `all(n>=3 for n in c.values())` — requiring ≥3 permutations per plan — but a plan with exactly 2 `##` sections (which the inclusion criterion '≥2 sections' permits) has only 2! = 2 distinct orderings, making the AC unfulfillable for that plan.
findings.location: Experiment B — mechanism section ('3 section-order permutations'); AC 'Exp B run recorded' prove-script `all(n>=3 for n in c.values())`
scenarios: An included plan has exactly 2 top-level sections; the AC prove-script fails because only 2 permutations exist.
Two implementors choose different permutation strategies (random vs rotation vs lexicographic), producing non-comparable order-shuffle results and defeating reproducibility.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the inclusion filter to '≥3 top-level `##` sections' (verify against `corpus_sample.json` to confirm N≥15 still holds), and specify permutation selection as: 'original order plus 2 additional orders sampled without replacement using `random.sample(permutations, 2, random.seed(42))`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cardinality assertion to the AC3 proving command: after loading `o`, assert that `len(o) == len([json.loads(l) for l in open('...e6_ordershuffle_inputs.jsonl')])` (output rows match input rows), or equivalently cross-check per-plan row counts match the inputs file.
evidence: The proving command reads `o=[json.loads(l) for l in open('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e6_ordershuffle.jsonl')]; assert all(r['verdict'] in {'PASS','BLOCK','INDETERMINATE'} for r in o)` — no `len(o)` check
The inputs file is checked for `len(c)>=15 and all(n>=3 for n in c.values())` but the output file count is unchecked
This matches VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (5): missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count (15 plans × 3 permutations)
findings.finding: The AC3 proving command for Experiment B verifies that all rows in `e6_ordershuffle.jsonl` carry valid verdict values, but does NOT assert that the output file has the expected number of rows (15 plans × 3 permutations = 45). An incomplete run — e.g., one that produced only 30 of 45 results — would silently pass the AC, because the assertion iterates over whatever rows exist without checking the total count.
findings.location: AC: 'Exp B run recorded (order-shuffle over the committed plan-text corpus)', proving command: `assert all(r['verdict'] in {'PASS','BLOCK','INDETERMINATE'} for r in o)`
scenarios: The harness crashes partway through, writing only a subset of results; the AC still passes because every written row has a valid verdict
A re-run exclusion loop silently exhausts retries for some permutations without writing rows; the AC passes without detecting missing coverage
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the one-liner: `pc=collections.Counter(r['plan_id'] for r in o); assert pc==c, f'output counts {pc} != input counts {c}'` — where `c` is already computed from the inputs file in the same command.

findings.finding: The proving command for AC3 (Exp B run recorded) validates that each input plan_id has ≥3 permutation entries but does NOT assert that the output file `e6_ordershuffle.jsonl` has a corresponding verdict row for every plan×permutation — a partial run (e.g., 15 plans × 1 verdict each) would pass the command.
findings.location: AC: 'Exp B run recorded' — proving command `python3 -c "... assert all(r['verdict'] in {'PASS','BLOCK','INDETERMINATE'} for r in o) ..."`
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the proving command: `oc=collections.Counter((r['plan_id'],r['permutation_id']) for r in o); assert set(oc.keys())=={(r['plan_id'],r['permutation_id']) for r in ins}, 'missing output rows'; print('OK', len(oc), 'plan×permutation verdicts recorded')`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where `fleiss_kappa`, `raw_agreement`, and `permute_sections` will live so `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` can import them — either as a small `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/e6_metrics.py` module, a standalone `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_metrics.py` with an explicit `conftest.py` sys.path addition, or inline in the test file itself.
evidence: ABSENCE: no import path for `fleiss_kappa`, `raw_agreement`, or `permute_sections` is specified in the plan
tests/unit/ listing: all existing tests import from `rebar.*` installed packages or relative fixtures; no test imports from `docs/experiments/`
Scope section: helpers described as 'in the driver' `e6_judge_reliability.py`, not in a `src/rebar` module or a standalone importable package
findings.finding: The unit test AC (`tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py`) names `fleiss_kappa`, `raw_agreement`, and `permute_sections` as symbols to test, but the plan never specifies the module these helpers live in nor how `tests/unit/` will import them. The harness driver `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py` is not on the Python path for `tests/unit/`; the existing test suite in `tests/unit/` imports from `rebar.*` (installed packages) or uses `conftest.py` path fixtures, not from the harnesses directory.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Pure agreement/permutation helpers are unit-tested (CI-collectable, no LLM). `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` asserts `fleiss_kappa` and `raw_agreement`…'
scenarios: CI runs `pytest tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` which attempts `from e6_judge_reliability import fleiss_kappa` (or similar) and fails with ModuleNotFoundError unless the harness dir is added to sys.path or the helpers are extracted to a proper importable location.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the three pure helpers into `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_metrics.py` and add a `conftest.py` fixture (or `sys.path.insert`) in `tests/unit/` or alongside the test that adds the harnesses directory to the path before import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Exp A execution recipe: `gate_source.gate_read_root` is not required by `three_pass.pass2_verify` (the harness uses subprocess/direct API, not `assert_gated`); replace the gate-session wiring with a plain `repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` argument to `pass2_verify`, and note that the session guard only applies if porting to the production `rebar.llm` agentic path.
evidence: three_pass.py:201-227: `_verify_agentic` calls `e2.run_tool(b.name, b.input, repo_root)` — subprocess-based, no `assert_gated` call
exp2_agentic.py:39-60: `run_tool` is pure subprocess/file I/O with no `rebar.llm` imports
src/rebar/llm/config.py:210-234: `assert_gated` only raises when called, and it's never called from the harness path
findings.finding: The plan claims the Exp A harness must wrap `pass2_verify` calls inside `gate_source.gate_read_root(handle)` because `config.assert_gated` guards the agentic path. But `three_pass.py`'s `_verify_agentic` (the actual agentic path called by `pass2_verify`) never calls `assert_gated` — it calls `e2.run_tool` directly via subprocess and the Anthropic API, completely bypassing the `rebar.llm` production gate-session infrastructure. The `gate_source` wiring described in the Exp A recipe has no effect on whether `pass2_verify` runs successfully.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section: 'the agentic Pass-2 path requires BOTH an explicit `repo_root` AND an active gate session (verified execution recipe)'; the pseudocode wrapping `gate_source.gate_read_root(handle)`
scenarios: A developer following the plan's recipe will add the `gate_source` wrapping boilerplate under the impression it is required for correctness, then be confused when omitting it also works — or worse, assume the session guard IS enforcing safety when it is not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact numeric retry cap per finding for Exp A vote collection (and analogously for Exp B permutations), and update the LLM-spend section to reflect the worst-case spend including retries.
evidence: 'capping retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row (never silently padded)'
No numeric retry cap is stated anywhere in the plan for either Exp A or Exp B.
findings.finding: The retry cap for Exp A (self-consistency votes) is mentioned in prose ('capping retries') but the actual cap value is never specified, leaving the harness behaviour undefined when a finding persistently fails to produce a substantive vote.
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Per-vote infra exclusion' subsection; Scope section
scenarios: If the LLM tier is degraded, a finding could loop indefinitely (no cap) or the cap defaults to some library default that is undocumented and untested.
A cap of 1 vs. 5 vs. 10 materially affects spend and wall-clock time — without specifying it, the LLM-spend honesty section's '150 agentic Pass-2 calls' upper bound is not actually bounded.
findings.suggested_fix: Add e.g. `MAX_RETRIES_PER_FINDING = 3` (giving up to 3 extra attempts before marking the finding excluded) and state this constant in both the plan prose and the harness code; update the spend figure to `150 + 3×N_excluded_findings` worst-case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the exact `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` values the Exp A harness uses in `pass3_decide`, grounded in the production gate's criterion postures for the G6/E4/T3 cohort (e.g. confirm `blocking_enabled=False` and `block_threshold=0.95` match the deployed gate's rubric registry for these criteria).
evidence: Plan pseudocode: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)` — ellipsis used verbatim for both arguments

three_pass.py:283: `def pass3_decide(verify, block_threshold=0.95, blocking_enabled=False)` — the function has real defaults, but the plan's explicit `...` placeholders signal the harness should supply specific values

The corpus is 'advisory-heavy' (advisory 47 / dropped 20 / block 7) — suggesting blocking_enabled=False is correct, but the plan never commits to this; an implementer could plausibly use blocking_enabled=True for G6/T3 which appear in CODEBASE_GROUNDED

The `decision` over (block/advisory/dropped/indeterminate) is the direct input to Fleiss' κ — a wrong threshold changes the category assignments and makes the reliability number meaningless as a proxy for the production gate
findings.finding: The Exp A pseudocode passes `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` as literal ellipsis placeholders when calling `three_pass.pass3_decide`. These are not documentation shorthands — they are load-bearing values that determine the `decision` output (block/advisory/dropped), which is the unit of agreement for Fleiss' κ. If the harness author supplies wrong values (e.g. `blocking_enabled=True` for a cohort where all criteria are advisory, or a non-standard block_threshold), the `decision` distribution will be distorted and the κ measurement will be unreliable regardless of judge stability. The plan does not state what values should be used.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section, pseudocode block: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: Implementer sets blocking_enabled=True for T3 (since T3 is in CODEBASE_GROUNDED and might be assumed blocking) — decisions that would be 'advisory' in production become 'block', inflating cross-run disagreement artificially
Implementer uses block_threshold=0.5 instead of 0.95 — 'advisory' findings with moderate confidence flip to 'block', producing a different decision distribution than the real gate uses
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Exp A judge-run section: 'The harness uses `blocking_enabled=False` and `block_threshold=0.95` for all findings in the G6/E4/T3 cohort, matching the advisory default_posture of all v1 criteria in the production registry (`three_pass.py:355-356`). These values are read from the rubric; the harness must confirm them via `G.load_criteria(...)`.' This removes the ambiguous `...` placeholders and grounds the invocation in the verified production values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concrete values for `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` in the Exp A execution recipe — either state the rubric-derived values for {G6,E4,T3} (e.g. `blocking_enabled=False` because all three are advisory-postured in v8, `block_threshold=0.95` default), or say 'load the rubric and look up per-criterion values as `run_three_pass` does at `three_pass.py:356`.'
evidence: Plan code snippet: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
`three_pass.py:283`: `def pass3_decide(verify, block_threshold=0.95, blocking_enabled=False)` — the decision rule `block` vs `advisory` vs `dropped` diverges based on these two values

`three_pass.py:356`: `thresholds = {c['id']: c.get('block_threshold', 0.95) for c in rubric}` — the normal call site reads these from the loaded rubric; the E6 recipe does not mention loading the rubric
ABSENCE: the plan never states 'load the rubric and look up block_threshold/blocking_enabled for each finding's criterion', nor gives concrete values for G6, E4, T3
findings.finding: The Exp A execution recipe passes `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` as Python ellipsis placeholders to `three_pass.pass3_decide`, but never specifies what values to substitute. `pass3_decide` uses these to determine whether a finding reaches `decision='block'` vs `'advisory'` — the only path to a non-advisory `decision` outcome — so the metric computed over the 3-vote runs depends on these inputs. The plan never says to load the rubric (as `run_three_pass` does at `three_pass.py:356`) nor what criterion-level posture/threshold applies to {G6,E4,T3} findings in the frozen corpus.
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Judge run' section, code snippet: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: Executor uses `block_threshold=0.95, blocking_enabled=False` (defaults) — advisory-heavy corpus never emits 'block', so the decision distribution is artificially skewed, and κ may be inflated by prevalence.
Executor uses `blocking_enabled=True` with the wrong threshold — different run produces different decision rates, making the 3-vote comparison non-comparable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the code snippet, replace `block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...` with `block_threshold=thresholds.get(inp['criterion'], 0.95), blocking_enabled=(postures.get(inp['criterion']) == 'blocking')` — and add a one-liner showing how `thresholds`/`postures` are loaded from the rubric file before the loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete retry cap to the Exp A per-vote infra-exclusion rule (e.g., 'retry up to 3 times per vote slot; if still indeterminate after 3 retries, mark the finding excluded and record it in `e6_selfconsistency.jsonl` with `excluded=true`').
evidence: Plan: 'E6 drops and re-runs any vote with `decision == "indeterminate"` until 3 substantive votes (block/advisory/dropped) are collected per finding, capping retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row'
ABSENCE: no numeric cap stated (e.g. 'max 3 retries per vote slot', 'max 9 total attempts per finding')
`_verify_agentic` in `three_pass.py:209` uses `range(8)` as its own internal iteration cap, but this is the agentic tool-use loop, not the E6 vote-retry loop
findings.finding: The retry cap for Exp A vote-collection is left unspecified. The plan says infra-INDETERMINATE votes are 'dropped and re-run … capping retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row', but never states what the cap is (e.g., 3 extra attempts, 5, until the cohort headroom is exhausted). An executor must invent this value, which affects the reported exclusion count and the effective N.
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Per-vote infra exclusion' section: 'caps retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row (never silently padded)'
scenarios: With a transient LLM outage, a cap of 2 excludes valid findings; a cap of 10 burns budget. Without a stated cap, two implementors produce different N and different reported exclusion rows, making the run non-reproducible.

findings.finding: Exp B specifies '3 section-order permutations' per plan but never defines how those 3 are selected from the N! possible orderings. The helper `permute_sections` is referenced but its selection protocol (random with seed, exhaustive up to 3, sorted enumeration, etc.) is unspecified — this is a decision rule co-located with the schema that is missing.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section: 'generate 3 section-order permutations by reordering its top-level `##` section blocks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'For plans with ≥3 sections, take permutations [1], [2], [3] of `list(itertools.permutations(sections))[1:4]` (lexicographic order, skipping the identity at index 0); for 2-section plans, use the single non-identity permutation three times (or exclude from the denominator). Fix `random.seed(1729)` if random selection is preferred.'

findings.finding: Exp A states findings are 'sampled deterministically (fixed seed)' but never states the seed value, making the N=50 cohort non-reproducible by a different executor. The existing project convention (SEED=1729 in `build_adjudication_corpus.py`) is not referenced or adopted.
findings.location: Experiment A — Sample frame: 'N = 50 findings, sampled deterministically (fixed seed) from the 74-member cohort'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Exp A sample frame: 'Sampled with `random.seed(1729)` (matching the project's `build_adjudication_corpus.py` convention) after deterministic sort by `finding_id`; the seed is recorded in the harness file header comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` in the Experiment A code snippet with their concrete production values (or a named constant with a cross-reference to where they are defined, e.g., `block_threshold=config.BLOCK_THRESHOLD` from `three_pass.py:NN`).
evidence: Verbatim plan code: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)` — both arguments are literal `...` (Python Ellipsis), not values or even named constants
The plan specifies that Pass-3 is deterministic given Pass-2, meaning the threshold values directly determine the `decision` output and the agreement metric being measured
No cross-reference is given to where these values are defined or what their production defaults are
findings.finding: The execution recipe code snippet for Experiment A passes `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` as literal Python ellipsis placeholders. An executing agent cannot determine the correct values for these parameters without stopping to investigate the codebase, which blocks faithful reproduction of the gate's production decision logic.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section, code snippet: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: An executor runs the harness with incorrect threshold values, producing a `decision` distribution that doesn't match the production gate, invalidating the self-consistency measurement
An executor stops to ask what values to use, requiring a round-trip to the author
findings.suggested_fix: Identify the production defaults (e.g., from `three_pass.py` or `config.py`) and state them explicitly: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v, block_threshold=0.7, blocking_enabled=True)` (values illustrative — use the actual production defaults).

findings.finding: The plan's exclusion rule for infra-INDETERMINATE verdicts in Exp B ('judge-produced infra-INDETERMINATE excluded, re-run') and Exp A ('no-verification runs excluded') references a detection signal (`llm_unavailable`) that exists only in `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`'s `coverage` dict — a path not used by the E6 harness. In the harness, API failures on `pass2_verify` return `{verify: None, mode: 'error:...'}`, and `pass3_decide(None)` emits `decision='indeterminate', reason='no-verification'` — structurally identical to a judge-produced INDETERMINATE. No field distinguishes infra failure from judge abstention in this code path.
findings.location: Grounded definitions section: 'Infra-INDETERMINATE (LLM tier unavailable, `llm_unavailable`) is not judge instability — such runs are excluded and re-run'; Experiment A judge run section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the E6 driver spec: 'Infra failure detection in the harness: for Exp A, a vote is infra-failed if the raw `pass2_verify` result has `mode` starting with `error:` or equals `agentic-no-verdict`; for Exp B, a permutation run is infra-failed if `finalize_verdict` raises or returns a coverage dict with `llm_unavailable=True`. Infra-failed votes/runs are logged separately, excluded from κ computation, and retried up to 2× before being flagged as unresolvable.'

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: robust
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `fleiss_kappa` implementation source and function signature: either 'hand-rolled (stdlib-only, consistent with `cohens_kappa` in `eval.py`) — input: `votes: list[list[str]]` (N findings × 3 votes), output: float' OR 'import from statsmodels (add to deps)'; and provide or cite the known-κ fixture value so the CI test is self-verifying.
evidence: Plan: '`tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` asserts `fleiss_kappa` and `raw_agreement` on hand-computed fixtures (incl. a perfect-agreement fixture → κ==1.0, raw==1.0, and a known-κ table)'
`src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:343`: existing `cohens_kappa` is 2-rater only — cannot be reused for 3-vote Fleiss' κ
ABSENCE: no function signature specified (e.g. `fleiss_kappa(votes: list[list[str]], categories: list[str]) -> float`), no input format stated, no dep policy stated
AC proving script: `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py -q` — will fail if `statsmodels` not installed and that was the chosen impl
findings.finding: `fleiss_kappa` is named as a 'pure helper' to be unit-tested with 'a known-κ table' fixture, but its implementation source, function signature, and input matrix format are never defined. The repo already has a hand-rolled `cohens_kappa` (2-rater, `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:343`) but Fleiss' κ requires a different algorithm (multi-rater count matrix). The plan doesn't specify whether to hand-roll it, import from `statsmodels`, or adapt the existing kappa helper — each has a different input schema (raw vote list vs n_subjects×n_categories count matrix).
findings.location: Scope section — `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py`; Acceptance Criteria — 'Pure agreement/permutation helpers are unit-tested'
scenarios: Executor hand-rolls Fleiss' κ with an incorrect formula (a common error: confusing the observed agreement formula with Cohen's); the 'known-κ table' fixture appears to pass if the fixture is not independently verified.
Executor imports `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa` which is not in rebar's deps — CI fails on import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an incremental/append-write checkpoint strategy to the harness (e.g., append each vote result to `e6_selfconsistency.jsonl` as it completes and skip already-recorded `(finding_id, vote_index)` pairs on restart) so a mid-run failure is recoverable without re-spending.
evidence: 'A = 50 findings × 3 votes = 150 agentic Pass-2 calls'
'B = 15 plans × 3 permutations = 45 full-gate runs'
No checkpoint, resume, or partial-output durability mechanism is described anywhere in the plan.
The plan says the harness 'writes the agreement tables + summary' — only at completion, not incrementally.
findings.finding: No intermediate checkpointing is specified for the Exp A 150-call or Exp B 45-call sequences; a mid-run failure forces a complete restart, wasting spend and violating the partial-state safety requirement.
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Judge run' subsection; Experiment B — 'Mechanism' subsection; LLM-spend honesty section
scenarios: The harness crashes at call 100/150; all 100 completed votes are lost and the operator must restart and pay for 150 more calls.
An LLM rate-limit pause causes a timeout at call 130; without checkpointing the operator cannot distinguish completed vs. not-yet-run findings.
findings.suggested_fix: Write each vote result as a JSONL append immediately after the call returns (rather than accumulating in memory and writing at the end); at startup, read any existing output file and skip findings/permutations already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `fleiss_kappa` should live in/extend `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py` (alongside the existing `cohens_kappa`) or remain experiment-local — and if experiment-local, explicitly justify why the established `eval.py` kappa home is not the right place.
evidence: `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:343`: `def cohens_kappa(rater_a: list, rater_b: list) -> float:` — hand-rolled, stdlib-only, with edge-case handling (degenerate constant rater, empty sequences).

`src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:20`: 'The pure logic here (spec validation, grader discipline, the gate, coverage, JUnit conversion, kappa) is stdlib-only and offline-testable' — the codebase has an established home for stdlib kappa logic.
Plan text: 'pure helper `fleiss_kappa`... unit-tested in `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py`' — new standalone, placement unspecified relative to existing kappa code.

ABSENCE: the plan never states whether `fleiss_kappa` extends `eval.py` or is a new parallel implementation, and never explains why a separate helper is preferred over extending the existing kappa module.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `fleiss_kappa` as a new standalone 'pure helper' without acknowledging that `cohens_kappa` already exists as a hand-rolled stdlib-only kappa implementation in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:343`. Fleiss' κ is mathematically distinct from Cohen's κ (handles k≥2 raters), so reuse is not trivial — but the plan never resolves whether `fleiss_kappa` should live in/alongside `eval.py` (extending the codebase's established kappa infrastructure) or as a standalone in the experiment harness, risking a second parallel kappa implementation that diverges from the existing one.
findings.location: Scope section / `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` AC / Experiment A metric definition
scenarios: An implementer writes a second standalone `fleiss_kappa` in the experiment harness; later `eval.py`'s `cohens_kappa` is updated (e.g. edge-case fix) but the parallel implementation is not, creating a silent divergence.
A future k=3 eval spec wants multi-rater kappa and discovers two divergent implementations — one in `eval.py`, one in the E6 harness — with no canonical authority.
findings.suggested_fix: If the helper is experiment-scoped (used only by E6), keep it local to the harness/test file and note that it is intentionally not promoted to `eval.py`. If it is intended as a reusable multi-rater generalisation, add it to `eval.py` alongside `cohens_kappa` (both are stdlib-only and offline-testable, satisfying the existing module contract).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `runs/e6_selfconsistency_agreement.csv` and `runs/e6_ordershuffle_agreement.csv` to the Scope section's runs/ deliverables enumeration so the deliverable list is self-contained and does not rely solely on forward-references to ACs.
evidence: Scope says: 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/ — committed inputs + outputs (all paths in the ACs below)' — the phrase 'all paths in the ACs below' is a forward-reference, not an enumeration
AC2 mandates: 'runs/e6_selfconsistency_agreement.csv (one row per finding: finding_id,criterion,decision_1,decision_2,decision_3,all_agree,modal_decision)'
AC4 mandates: 'runs/e6_ordershuffle_agreement.csv (one row per plan: plan_id,verdict_1,verdict_2,verdict_3,all_agree,modal_verdict)'
Neither CSV appears in the Scope section's bullet list of named deliverables.
findings.finding: The Scope section's enumeration of runs/ deliverables omits `runs/e6_selfconsistency_agreement.csv` and `runs/e6_ordershuffle_agreement.csv`, even though both are concrete output files mandated by ACs 2 and 4 respectively. An implementer reading Scope as the authoritative build list would not know to produce them.
findings.location: Scope section ('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/ — committed inputs + outputs') vs. AC 'Exp A passes its floor' and AC 'Exp B passes its floor'
scenarios: An implementer writing the harness uses the Scope section as the delivery checklist and skips the per-finding/per-plan agreement CSVs, only producing the summary JSON — the ACs then fail at review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the T3 row count in `adjudication_corpus.jsonl`; if zero, either (a) update the sample-frame description to state the cohort is G6+E4 (N rows) and explain why T3 absence is acceptable for R5 gating, or (b) supplement the sample by sourcing T3 findings from live gate runs before the experiment executes.
evidence: Text search of docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/adjudication_corpus.jsonl for '"criterion": "T3"' returns zero matches.
T3 is an overlay criterion (criteria_v8.json: 'routing: overlay') that fires only when an external integration is present — most plans in the corpus will not trigger it, explaining the absence.
The plan states the cohort exists and 'clears the N≥50 floor with 24 rows of headroom' but the claimed total (74) is the G6+E4 headcount, not a verified three-criterion total.
sota:195-199 defines R5's routing cohort as G6/E4/T3; if T3 has no corpus rows, E6 does not validate the T3 leg of R5's sub-answer.
findings.finding: The plan's Exp A sample frame states '74 rows across 66 distinct tickets' for `criterion ∈ {G6, E4, T3}`, but a full-text search of the committed `adjudication_corpus.jsonl` finds zero rows with `criterion: T3`. The actual cohort is G6+E4 only. This breaks (a) the stated count of 74 (which is therefore the G6+E4 count, not a three-criterion count), and (b) the plan's key argument that the sample is 'exactly the R5 sub-answer's own routing cohort' — R5 routes G6/E4/T3, but E6 would actually measure only G6/E4, leaving T3 unmeasured.
findings.location: Experiment A — Sample frame (concrete) section: '74 rows across 66 distinct tickets (advisory 47 / dropped 20 / block 7)'
scenarios: The harness filters on `criterion in {G6, E4, T3}` but effectively samples only G6+E4 rows, so the experiment measures a two-criterion judge rather than the three-criterion cohort R5 depends on.
If a reviewer counts 74 rows expecting T3 representation and uses that to argue E6 validates R5's full routing cohort, the argument is unsupported.
findings.suggested_fix: Run: `grep -c '"criterion": "T3"' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/adjudication_corpus.jsonl`. If zero, update the plan's sample-frame paragraph to read 'criterion ∈ {G6, E4}' (dropping T3), state the actual N, and add a sentence explaining that T3 is an overlay criterion with insufficient corpus coverage — and assess whether R5's T3 sub-path requires a separate small sample or can be deferred.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `fleiss_kappa` implementation source: either (a) declare it will be hand-rolled and co-located with / extending the existing `cohens_kappa` in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py`, or (b) name the library (e.g. `statsmodels`) and add it to `pyproject.toml` under an appropriate extra — and in either case, confirm the 'pure helper, no LLM, CI-collectable' constraint is met.
evidence: Plan text: 'tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py asserts fleiss_kappa and raw_agreement on hand-computed fixtures (incl. a perfect-agreement fixture → κ==1.0, raw==1.0, and a known-κ table)'
Plan text: '(raters = the 3 interchangeable votes; subjects = findings; categories = the 4-value decision space)' — Fleiss' κ is the selected metric but no implementation source is named

eval.py:21: 'The pure logic here (spec validation, grader discipline, the gate, coverage, JUnit conversion, kappa) is stdlib-only and offline-testable' — established prior art in the codebase for hand-rolled kappa
eval.py:343: existing hand-rolled `cohens_kappa` — a 2-rater version of the same family of statistic
pyproject.toml: `statsmodels` is absent from all dependency groups (core, agents, eval, dev)
ABSENCE: the plan never states 'hand-roll' or 'import from statsmodels' or 'extend eval.py'
findings.finding: The plan designates `fleiss_kappa` as a 'pure helper' (implying offline/stdlib implementation) and specifies it must pass CI-collectable unit tests with a 'known-κ table', but never resolves WHERE the implementation comes from. The canonical third-party source (`statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa`) is entirely absent from `pyproject.toml`; the project's established pattern is hand-rolled stdlib-only kappa (the existing `cohens_kappa` in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:343`). The plan also introduces a parallel agreement-metric module in the new harness without explaining why the Fleiss' κ logic isn't co-located with (or extending) the existing kappa machinery in `eval.py`, which already serves the same measurement role. This is both an unverified capability assertion ('pure helper' with no specified implementation) and a potential anti-repackaging issue.
findings.location: Scope section — `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py`; AC: 'Pure agreement/permutation helpers are unit-tested (CI-collectable, no LLM)'
scenarios: An implementer adds `statsmodels` as a new dependency (not in pyproject.toml), breaking the project's lean-runtime discipline and the 'no LLM' CI-collectable constraint if `statsmodels` isn't installed in the test environment.
An implementer hand-rolls Fleiss' κ independently, creating a duplicate of the pattern already in eval.py, diverging from the codebase's established location for inter-rater statistics.

The unit test references a fixture with a 'known-κ table' but the implementation produces a slightly different result due to a hand-roll edge case (degenerate category, skewed base rates), and there is no ground-truth reference implementation to validate against.
findings.suggested_fix: Hand-roll `fleiss_kappa` as a stdlib-only function following the same pattern as `cohens_kappa` in `eval.py`, add it there (or import it from there in the harness), and note this lineage in the plan. This eliminates the new dependency and the anti-repackaging risk in one step.

findings.finding: Experiment B's 'full gate verdict path' claim is not achievable via `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` alone from a standalone harness. `finalize_verdict` requires a `PlanContext` dataclass and a pre-partitioned `parts` dict (`{blocking, surfaced, overflow, indeterminate, dropped}`) that are produced deep inside the production workflow. The harness `three_pass.run_three_pass()` returns a flat list of per-finding decision dicts — not the `parts` shape `finalize_verdict` expects.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section: 'run the full gate verdict path (Pass-1 → Pass-2 → Pass-3 → finalize_verdict)'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `repo_root` is supplied to `three_pass.pass2_verify(agentic=True)` during Exp A re-judging. The agentic path only activates when `repo_root` is non-empty AND the criterion is in `CODEBASE_GROUNDED`. The frozen input file `e6_selfconsistency_inputs.jsonl` stores `{finding_id, ticket_id, title, plan, criterion, finding, location, suggested_fix}` — no `repo_root` field. Without a valid repo_root at judge-run time, `pass2_verify` silently falls back to single-turn mode even when `agentic=True` is passed.
findings.location: Experiment A — Re-judge inputs are frozen section and Judge run section: 'three_pass.pass2_verify(title, plan, finding, repo_root, agentic=True)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"repo_root": "/path/to/rebar"` to each frozen input row and document that E6 must be run from a checkout of the rebar repo; alternatively, pin a specific git SHA and use the snapshot read path.

findings.finding: Exp B specifies running the 'full gate verdict path (Pass-1 → Pass-2 → Pass-3 → `finalize_verdict`)' to obtain `verdict ∈ {PASS,BLOCK,INDETERMINATE}`, but the existing harness entry point `three_pass.run_three_pass()` returns per-finding decision records — not the aggregate verdict — and `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` requires a `PlanContext`, a `parts` partition dict (`blocking`, `surfaced`, `overflow`, `indeterminate`, `dropped`), `coaching`, `coverage`, `runner_name`, and `model` arguments that no existing harness assembles. The plan provides no wiring spec for how `e6_judge_reliability.py` will bridge `run_three_pass` output into `finalize_verdict` inputs.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section: 'run the full gate verdict path (Pass-1 → Pass-2 → Pass-3 → `finalize_verdict`)'; Scope section: '`docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py`'

findings.finding: The production Pass-2 verifier has been retired and replaced: `three_pass.pass2_verify` is a harness-only function, not the current production verifier. The production gate now routes through `rebar.llm.review_kernel.verify_findings` (via the engine workflow). Exp A measures the harness verifier's reliability, but the plan frames it as measuring 'the existing gate's' judge stability — this is a mislabeled scope.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section: 'three_pass.pass2_verify(title, plan, finding, repo_root, agentic=True)' and 'the agentic Pass-2 verifier ... the R5 cohort's plan_review_verifier_agentic path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the lifecycle of the throwaway store clone: specify that it is created in a `tempfile.mkdtemp()`-managed directory, used within a `try/finally` block (or Python context manager), and unconditionally deleted on exit whether success or failure.
evidence: 'set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone of the tickets store>'
'Only the throwaway REBAR_TRACKER_DIR clone is edited — the real tickets store is never written.'
No cleanup, `finally` block, `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`, or post-run deletion is mentioned anywhere.
findings.finding: The throwaway store clone created for Exp B has no defined retirement/cleanup path: who deletes the temp directory, when, and what happens if the harness crashes leaving the clone behind are all unspecified.
findings.location: Experiment B — 'Mechanism' subsection
scenarios: Harness crashes after creating the clone; the clone accumulates across repeated runs, consuming disk space and potentially confusing future runs that happen to reuse the same path.
A second concurrent E6 run picks up the leftover clone from a prior failed run, getting stale/wrong plan text and producing silently corrupt results.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory()` as a context manager in the harness: `with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir: shutil.copytree(REAL_TRACKER_DIR, tmpdir); os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR'] = tmpdir; ...` — this guarantees cleanup on any exit path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the agreement floors (κ_floor=0.6, raw_floor=0.8) as named constants in the harness (`e6_judge_reliability.py`) and reference them from both ACs' proving scripts and the README rather than inlining the literals in four places.
evidence: 'PASS iff κ ≥ 0.6 (Landis–Koch "substantial") AND raw agreement ≥ 0.8' appears verbatim in both Exp A and Exp B metric sections
'd["pass"]==(d["fleiss_kappa"]>=0.6 and d["raw_agreement"]>=0.8)' is inlined into two separate AC proving-scripts
The README AC also requires the floors be written there, creating a fourth site
findings.finding: The pass/fail thresholds (κ≥0.6 AND raw_agreement≥0.8) that gate downstream stories R3 and R5 are duplicated across at least four locations — the Exp A metric section, the Exp B metric section, two AC proving-scripts, and the expected README — with no single named constant or config dict. If the team revisits these floors (e.g., after observing high base-rate skew), every site must be updated consistently.
findings.location: Experiment A — Metric + floor section; Experiment B — Metric + floor section; Acceptance Criteria (AC 2 proving script, AC 4 proving script)
scenarios: A reviewer decides κ≥0.7 is more appropriate after seeing the skewed advisory-heavy distribution; they must update the harness constant, both AC scripts, and the README — a high risk of inconsistency.
findings.suggested_fix: In `e6_judge_reliability.py` add `KAPPA_FLOOR = 0.6; RAW_FLOOR = 0.8` and write `e6_summary.json` with `{"kappa_floor": KAPPA_FLOOR, "raw_floor": RAW_FLOOR, ...}`. AC proving-scripts then read `d['kappa_floor']` and `d['raw_floor']` for the comparison, eliminating hardcoded literals from the scripts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Exp B execution recipe: replace 'force=True defeats idempotent review-reuse' with the accurate explanation that `sign=False` is what bypasses idempotent reuse (the guard is `if sign and not force:` at `__init__.py:508`), and that `force=True` is redundant (though harmless) in the `sign=False` recipe.
evidence: Plan asserts: 'sign=False / emit_sidecar=False keep the run non-mutating (no attestation, no REVIEW_RESULT event) and force=True defeats idempotent review-reuse so every permutation is judged fresh (__init__.py:508-518, 570-587)'
__init__.py:508: `if sign and not force:` — the entire idempotent-reuse check is inside `if sign`, so when sign=False it is skipped unconditionally; force is never evaluated in that path
__init__.py:517: `if sign:` — drift-refresh similarly gated on sign only, force is irrelevant
The cited lines 508-518 contain the guard that contradicts the plan's stated mechanism
findings.finding: The Exp B execution recipe claims 'force=True defeats idempotent review-reuse so every permutation is judged fresh' citing __init__.py:508-518, but the actual guard at line 508 is `if sign and not force:` — when sign=False (as the recipe uses), the idempotent-reuse block is unconditionally skipped regardless of force. The force flag only matters when sign=True. The plan misidentifies force=True as the active mechanism for defeating reuse when it is sign=False that gates the entire block.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section; recipe line `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True`; cited `__init__.py:508-518`
scenarios: A reader wiring up the harness for Exp B following the plan's explanation may add `force=True` believing it is the correctness-critical parameter, while the actual correctness depends solely on `sign=False`.

If someone later changes the harness to `sign=True` (e.g., to capture attestation data) without understanding that force=True is now load-bearing, they could inadvertently reintroduce idempotent-reuse skipping and get stale results for unchanged plans.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the recipe comment to: `sign=False` suppresses idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh (both are gated on `if sign:` at `__init__.py:508,517`); `force=True` is redundant here but left for clarity. Remove the citation of `508-518` as the mechanism for force's effect.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
